content.text: What is Caching for Cost Reduction?
Every call to an LLM API incurs a token cost. In agentic systems, a large fraction of those tokens are

: the system prompt that defines the agent's role, the full set of tool definitions, and any reference documents loaded at startup. Sending that same static prefix on every turn of a multi-step conversation wastes money and adds latency. Caching is the practice of storing that prefix on the provider's infrastructure — or in a client-side store — so subsequent requests pay a fraction of the normal input cost.
(also called server-side prefix caching). Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google each support a variant. On Anthropic's API, you mark a cache breakpoint by adding
to the last content block you want cached. The server stores the KV (key-value) state for that prefix for five minutes. Subsequent requests with the same prefix pay
the normal input rate for the cached tokens, at the cost of a one-time
write surcharge on the first miss.

(client-side). Instead of caching the KV state of the prompt, you cache complete LLM responses keyed by an embedding similarity score of the incoming query. If a new query is semantically close to a past query, the stored response is returned without contacting the LLM at all. This is independent of the API provider and can reduce costs to near-zero for repetitive query patterns, but requires managing an embedding store and accepting that the returned answer may not reflect the current date or context.

— hashing the full request and storing the response in Redis or a similar store. This only helps when the identical request is repeated, making it most useful for RAG pipelines and batch evaluation workloads rather than interactive agents.

LLM API costs scale with token volume. A system prompt that defines an agent's persona, lists twenty tool schemas, and embeds a one-thousand-word policy document can easily run to 3,000–6,000 input tokens. If that agent handles 10,000 conversations per day, the fixed prefix alone generates 30–60 million input tokens daily. At
pricing (~$3 per million input tokens), that is $90–$180 per day, every day, in overhead that has nothing to do with the user's actual query.

Prompt caching reduces those cached tokens to 10% of their uncached cost. At scale that turns a five-figure monthly line item into a four-figure one. For organizations running multi-agent pipelines — where an orchestrator repeatedly calls sub-agents each with their own large context — the compound savings are larger still.

Beyond pure cost, caching improves latency. KV cache hits bypass the most compute-intensive part of the prefill phase (the attention over the cached tokens), reducing time-to-first-token for long-context requests. This is observable: a 4,000-token system prompt that would add 800–1,200 ms to time-to-first-token on a cold request may add only 50–100 ms on a cache hit.

Cache hits cost 10% of the normal input rate on Anthropic. A 5,000-token system prompt that hits cache 90% of the time effectively costs 0.1 × 0.9 + 1.0 × 0.1 = 0.19× its uncached price across the request population.

The prefill phase for cached tokens is skipped at the compute level. Time-to-first-token shrinks significantly for requests with large static prefixes, improving perceived responsiveness for end users.

Once a large document or knowledge base snippet is cached, including it becomes almost free. Teams can afford to keep a full product manual, legal policy, or code repository context in the system prompt rather than truncating it to save money.

A single cache write serves all concurrent requests that share the same prefix within the TTL. Higher traffic means a higher cache hit rate, so marginal cost per request falls as usage grows — the opposite of a system where every request pays full price.
Prompt caching requires only adding a
field to the API request. The response shape is unchanged; the API returns
so you can measure effectiveness without restructuring the agent.
Caching is not free and is not universally applicable. Each technique carries a distinct set of constraints.

Anthropic requires at least 1,024 tokens (for Haiku) or 2,048 tokens (for Sonnet and Opus) in the cached prefix to use prompt caching. Prompts shorter than this cannot be cached, so the technique is only effective for genuinely large system prompts.

Anthropic's ephemeral cache expires after five minutes of inactivity. If your traffic is bursty — a few requests per hour rather than a steady stream — the cache will expire between requests and every call pays the write surcharge. Monitor your cache hit rate in production; a sub-50% hit rate means your traffic pattern does not justify caching costs.
Cache invalidation on any prefix change.

The cache key is determined by the exact content of the prefix up to the breakpoint. Inserting the current date, a user-specific greeting, or a dynamic context string before the tool definitions invalidates the cache on every request. The cached content must be byte-for-byte identical between requests.

Cache creation costs 1.25× the normal input rate. A cache miss (new cache population) is more expensive than an uncached request. The break-even point — the minimum number of cache hits needed to cover the write premium — is small (about two hits), but must be considered for very low-traffic endpoints.

Returning a cached response to a semantically similar but not identical query can surface outdated information. The similarity threshold requires careful tuning: too tight and the cache hit rate is negligible; too loose and the agent gives wrong answers.
Each provider implements caching differently. Anthropic uses explicit
markers. OpenAI caches automatically for prompts over 1,024 tokens on compatible models. Google Gemini uses a separate
resource. Migrating between providers requires adapter work.

Anthropic's prompt caching operates at the KV-cache level of the Transformer inference engine. When the model processes an input, the attention mechanism produces a key-value matrix for each token — this is the KV cache. Normally, the KV cache is discarded after each request. With prompt caching enabled, the server serializes and stores the KV state for all tokens up to the marked breakpoint, keyed by a hash of that exact token sequence.

On the next request, the server checks whether its prefix — up to the cache breakpoint — matches a stored KV snapshot. If it does (a cache hit), the prefill step skips over those tokens entirely: their KV values are loaded directly from storage and merged with the KV computation for the non-cached suffix (the user's current message). The model then runs generation as normal.
The cache is keyed on the
. Any change — even a single character — in the content before the breakpoint produces a different hash and a cache miss. The cached region must appear at the start of the message sequence:
content (such as a large document), then the dynamic user query last. Content after the breakpoint (the live user message, conversation history) is always processed fresh.
object in every Anthropic response includes two new fields when caching is configured:
— tokens written to the cache on a miss (billed at 1.25×).
— tokens served from cache on a hit (billed at 0.1×).
field covers the non-cached suffix (user message, conversation history), billed at the normal rate.

attrs.alt: Prompt caching architecture showing Agent/Orchestrator sending requests to a Cache Layer. Cache hits return immediately at 10% of normal cost; cache misses forward to the LLM API at full cost, then store the result for future hits.

content.text: The diagram shows the two paths a request can take. On a

(left branch), the provider's Cache Layer matches the incoming prefix against stored KV state and returns output without forwarding the request to the full inference engine. Cached tokens are billed at 0.1× the normal input rate. On a

(right branch), the request reaches the LLM API, which processes all tokens at full cost, then stores the KV state for the prefix so subsequent requests can hit the cache. The cache write is billed at 1.25× for the cached region only. Non-cached tokens (the user's current message and conversation history) always flow through the LLM API at the normal input rate regardless of the cache state.
Prompt caching is most valuable when the same static content appears at the beginning of many API calls within a short time window.

your system prompt is large (at least 2,048 tokens for Sonnet/Opus) and does not change between requests. Customer support bots with detailed product guides, coding assistants with full API references, and document QA systems that embed an entire PDF all fit this pattern.

you have twenty or more tool definitions. Each tool schema can run to 100–300 tokens; twenty tools add 2,000–6,000 tokens to every request. These definitions never change mid-session, making them ideal cache candidates.

running a multi-turn agent conversation. The conversation history grows with each turn, but the system prompt and tool list are constant. Cache the constant prefix; let the growing history accrue in the non-cached suffix.

your workload has many semantically equivalent queries — the same question worded differently by different users. FAQ bots and search-assist features see high duplication across users that prompt caching alone does not address.
Do not rely on caching when

your system prompt is dynamic — if you interpolate the current date, a user-specific context, or a session ID into the text that precedes your cache breakpoint, every request misses the cache. Move dynamic content after the breakpoint.
Do not rely on caching when

your traffic is very sparse. With Anthropic's 5-minute TTL, requests spaced more than five minutes apart will always cold-start. The write surcharge makes sparse caching more expensive than no caching.

Identify the static prefix. Audit your typical request: what content is identical across all calls? Usually: the system prompt, the full tool list, and any fixed reference documents. What changes per call? The user's latest message and the growing conversation history.

Ensure the static prefix meets the minimum token requirement. Count the tokens in your intended cached region. If it is under 2,048 tokens for Sonnet or 1,024 for Haiku, consider expanding the system prompt or concatenating additional stable context (a FAQ document, a schema definition) until you cross the threshold.
breakpoint. On Anthropic's API, mark the last block you want cached by setting
on that content block. For a system prompt, this is the system parameter's last block. For a document loaded into the user turn, it is the last block of that document content.

Keep the cached prefix stable. Do not alter the content between requests. Even a whitespace change invalidates the cache. Build the system prompt string once at application startup and reuse the same object.

Append dynamic content after the breakpoint. The user's current message, conversation history, and any per-request context must appear after the cache marker. The API processes these at full cost, but they are typically short compared to the cached prefix.
from every API response. Track your hit rate. A healthy deployment should see
dominating once the cache warms up. If
is consistently high, your prefix is changing between calls.

Set a warm-up strategy for bursty workloads. If your agent starts up and then receives traffic, send a "warm-up" request containing only the system prompt (no user message) at startup to populate the cache before real traffic arrives.
Basic: Enabling Prompt Caching on the System Prompt

# pip install anthropic
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Build a large, stable system prompt (must exceed 2,048 tokens for claude-sonnet).
# In practice this would be a real product guide or policy document.
SYSTEM_PROMPT = """
You are a customer support agent for Acme Corp. Your responsibilities:
- Answer questions about our SaaS platform
- Help users troubleshoot common errors
- Escalate billing disputes to the billing team

[... imagine 3,000 more tokens of product documentation here ...]
"""

def ask(user_message: str) -> anthropic.Message:
    """Send a message with prompt caching enabled on the system prompt."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=[
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": SYSTEM_PROMPT,
                # Mark this block as cacheable.
                # All content up to this point will be stored server-side.
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},
            }
        ],
        messages=[
            # This goes AFTER the cache breakpoint — not cached, always fresh.
            {"role": "user", "content": user_message}
        ],
    )
    return response

# First call: cache miss — the prefix is written to cache (1.25x cost).
r1 = ask("How do I reset my password?")
print(r1.usage)  # cache_creation_input_tokens > 0, cache_read_input_tokens == 0

# Second call (within 5 minutes): cache hit — cached tokens billed at 0.1x.
r2 = ask("What are your support hours?")
print(r2.usage)  # cache_read_input_tokens > 0, cache_creation_input_tokens == 0
Intermediate: Caching Tool Definitions in a Multi-Turn Agent

# Multi-turn agent that caches a large tool list across all turns.
import anthropic
from typing import Any

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# 20 tool schemas — these never change across requests.
# Shown abbreviated; each real tool schema adds 150-400 tokens.
TOOLS: list[dict[str, Any]] = [
    {
        "name": "web_search",
        "description": "Search the web for current information.",
        "input_schema": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "query": {"type": "string", "description": "The search query."},
                "max_results": {"type": "integer", "default": 5},
            },
            "required": ["query"],
        },
    },
    {
        "name": "read_file",
        "description": "Read the contents of a file from the workspace.",
        "input_schema": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "path": {"type": "string", "description": "Absolute path to the file."},
            },
            "required": ["path"],
        },
    },
    # ... 18 more tools ...
]

SYSTEM = "You are a research assistant with access to many tools."

def run_agent_turn(
    conversation: list[dict[str, Any]],
    new_user_message: str,
) -> tuple[str, list[dict[str, Any]]]:
    """
    Send one turn of a multi-turn conversation with prompt caching.
    Returns (assistant_text, updated_conversation).
    """
    conversation = conversation + [{"role": "user", "content": new_user_message}]

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
        max_tokens=2048,
        system=[
            {
                "type": "text",
                "text": SYSTEM,
                # Cache the system prompt prefix.
                "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},
            }
        ],
        tools=TOOLS,
        # Tool definitions are also cached if they appear before the
        # cache_control breakpoint in the effective token sequence.
        # On Anthropic, tool definitions are serialized AFTER the system
        # prompt, so they get cached when the system prompt is marked.
        messages=conversation,
    )

    assistant_text = "".join(
        block.text for block in response.content if hasattr(block, "text")
    )
    conversation = conversation + [{"role": "assistant", "content": response.content}]

    # Log cache efficiency for monitoring.
    u = response.usage
    hit = getattr(u, "cache_read_input_tokens", 0)
    miss = getattr(u, "cache_creation_input_tokens", 0)
    print(f"  cache hit={hit} miss={miss} uncached={u.input_tokens}")

    return assistant_text, conversation


# Simulate a three-turn conversation.
history: list[dict[str, Any]] = []
for user_msg in [
    "What is the current Python version?",
    "What changed in that version?",
    "Show me a code example using the new syntax.",
]:
    answer, history = run_agent_turn(history, user_msg)
    print(f"User: {user_msg}")
    print(f"Agent: {answer[:120]}...\n")

A legal research firm built an agentic QA system over their contract archive. The system prompt embeds a 4,500-token summary of company-wide contract standards, plus twelve tool definitions for querying their document database. Before enabling prompt caching, each of their analysts' 200 daily queries generated 4,500 input tokens just for the static prefix — 900,000 tokens per day at full price.

After enabling prompt caching, the first request each hour pays the 1.25× write surcharge and subsequent requests pay 0.1×. With their query pattern (steady stream during business hours, quiet at night), they see roughly 85% cache hit rates during working hours. Their effective input cost for the static prefix dropped from 100% to approximately (0.85 × 0.1) + (0.15 × 1.25) = 27% of the original — a 73% reduction.
The key structural decision is that
and the tool list are both included in the system message before the
breakpoint. The user's actual question arrives in the
array, after the breakpoint, and is never cached. Each call to
logs whether the call was a cache hit or miss, enabling ongoing monitoring of cache effectiveness.
Placing Dynamic Content Before the Cache Breakpoint
The most common and most costly mistake is including any per-request variable content — a timestamp, a user's name, a session UUID — inside the text that is marked with

. Every request generates a different prefix, and every request misses the cache. The write surcharge is then paid on every call without any offsetting hits, making caching strictly more expensive than not caching. Audit your system prompt string carefully: no
Attempting to cache a 1,000-token system prompt against Sonnet's 2,048-token minimum silently fails. The API accepts the request, bills it at the normal rate (not the write surcharge), and returns
. Teams that do not monitor usage fields may run for weeks believing caching is active when it is not. Always check the
fields in your first request after enabling caching.
Enabling caching is not the same as caching working. A healthy agent with busy traffic should see

composing 80–95% of the total cached input on a per-minute aggregate. If that number drops, it signals that the prefix is changing (a code deploy altered the system prompt) or that traffic has become too sparse for the TTL. Set up a metric and an alert: if the cache hit rate falls below 50%, investigate.
Assuming the Cache Is Shared Across Concurrent Instances

Prompt caching is per-model-instance on Anthropic's infrastructure. During periods of horizontal scaling when requests route to different server instances, each instance maintains its own KV cache. The first request to each instance is a miss. This is normal behavior, but it means that a 10× traffic spike that spins up new instances will cause a transient increase in cache misses. Do not treat a spike in
during a scale event as a bug.
Put the most stable content first.

Order your system prompt: role description, then product documentation, then tool schemas. Place the cache breakpoint after the last stable element. Anything that might change in the future should go after the breakpoint.
Build the system prompt string once at process startup.
Store the fully-rendered string in a module-level constant. Do not regenerate it on each request. If the string is generated from templates or loaded from files, do so once during
and cache the result as an instance attribute.
to your observability platform. Compute a rolling hit rate and alert if it degrades. This is the fastest way to detect that a deploy accidentally invalidated the cache.
Combine prompt caching with conversation history management.

Cache the static prefix; actively trim the conversation history to control non-cached token growth. A cached prefix of 4,000 tokens plus an unbounded history that grows to 20,000 tokens still produces expensive calls. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy for history.
Use multiple cache breakpoints for layered content.

markers per request. Use this to cache at multiple levels: system prompt (most stable), plus a large reference document (slightly less stable), plus the first several turns of conversation history (frequently reused in long sessions).

Whenever you deploy a new version of your agent — even if only the application code changed, not the prompt — send a warm-up request before routing real traffic. A single request containing the full system prompt (with no user message, or a trivial one) populates the cache on the new deployment.
Test caching in staging with production-like traffic rates.

Cache effectiveness depends on request frequency relative to TTL. A staging environment that sends one request per minute will show 0% hit rate even if production (with hundreds of requests per minute) shows 95%. Do not use staging cache metrics to predict production cache metrics.

The cost impact of prompt caching is multiplicative with traffic volume. At low volume (tens of requests per day) the write surcharge may exceed savings. The break-even calculation is: if the cached prefix has

tokens, each cache miss costs 1.25N token-equivalents and each hit costs 0.1N. The cache pays for itself after the second hit in the same TTL window (1 miss + 2 hits = 1.25 + 0.2 = 1.45 vs 3 uncached = 3.0). At production scale with hundreds of requests per minute, the hit rate approaches near-100% and the effective cost for the cached region approaches 0.1× with negligible write overhead.

Latency improvements compound with prefix size. At 2,000 cached tokens, the prefill speedup may be 200–400 ms. At 10,000 cached tokens (common for agents with many tools and a large knowledge base), the prefill speedup can exceed one second. For streaming responses (the common pattern in interactive agents), this is a direct reduction in time-to-first-token, improving perceived responsiveness.

Each Anthropic server instance maintains its own KV cache. During scale-out events, new instances start with empty caches and take one request each to warm up. At high throughput this overhead is negligible; at very low throughput it can dominate. Design your load balancer to prefer routing to warmed instances where possible, using sticky sessions based on system prompt hash.

If your orchestrator calls multiple models (e.g., Haiku for routing decisions, Sonnet for generation), each model maintains its own cache. Caching the Haiku system prompt separately from the Sonnet system prompt requires

For capacity planning, compute expected monthly savings as: (average cached tokens per request) × (requests per month) × (hit rate) × (normal input price) × (1 - 0.1). Subtract the write overhead: (cached tokens per request) × (requests per month) × (miss rate) × (normal input price) × (1.25 - 1.0). The net savings are the difference.
Set cache_control on system or user content blocks. Returns usage.cache_read_input_tokens and usage.cache_creation_input_tokens.
Automatic for prompts over 1,024 tokens on gpt-4o and o-series. Returns usage.prompt_tokens_details.cached_tokens. No explicit marker required.
Create a CachedContent resource with a configurable TTL. Reference its name in subsequent GenerateContent calls. Billed separately at a storage rate while cached.
Open-source library that sits in front of any LLM client. Stores response by embedding similarity. Works across providers. Requires managing an embedding model and a vector store.
set_llm_cache(InMemoryCache()) or RedisCache(). Only hits on identical prompts. Useful for batch evaluation where the same prompts repeat exactly.
Export cache_read_input_tokens and cache_creation_input_tokens as counters. Build a hit-rate dashboard. Alert on sustained drop below threshold.
KV cache stored on provider infrastructure
Low — one marker in the API request
Large static system prompts, tool definitions, reference docs
Store full LLM responses by embedding similarity
High — embedding model, vector store, staleness policy
Long conversation histories, RAG retrieval snippets
Route cheap queries to a smaller model

Prompt caching and semantic caching are complementary, not mutually exclusive. A production system might use prompt caching to amortize the static prefix cost, semantic caching to short-circuit repeated user queries, and model downgrading for low-complexity routing decisions — layering all three reduces cost at every dimension.

Prompt caching stores the serialized KV state of your system prompt on provider infrastructure. The same trust boundary applies as to the requests themselves: Anthropic encrypts data in transit and at rest, and cached data is isolated per account. However, two operational security concerns are worth considering.
Sensitive data in the cached prefix.

If your system prompt embeds a user's personal information, authentication tokens, or internal business data, that data persists in the provider's KV store for five minutes after the last request. For most compliance scenarios this is acceptable (the data was already being sent with every request), but audit your system prompt for data that should not leave your infrastructure at all, and remove it before caching.

Prompt caching is scoped to your Anthropic account and API key. Different customers' requests do not share a cache — the provider guarantees isolation. Within your own system, however, if multiple agents share the same API key and happen to produce the same prefix (unlikely, but possible if they use the same system prompt template), they will share a cache entry. This is harmless in most cases, but be aware of it if you use different system prompts to enforce different security policies for different user tiers.

Your agent must function correctly on both cache hits and cache misses. The cache expiring mid-session is normal. Do not write code that assumes a cache hit — the API always returns a valid response regardless of cache state, so no special handling is needed. The only observable difference is cost and latency, not correctness.

If your agent includes user-supplied content inside the cached prefix (for example, a user-chosen persona or configuration string embedded in the system prompt), a malicious user could craft a string that alters agent behavior for all subsequent users who share the same cache entry. Never include user-supplied content before the cache breakpoint. All user input must go into the non-cached suffix.

If you roll back a deployment that changed the system prompt, the old cache entry expires naturally within five minutes. There is no need to manually invalidate the cache. However, if you urgently need to clear a cached prompt (for example, after a prompt injection is discovered), rotating the API key effectively forces a cache miss on all subsequent requests, since the new key has no warm cache.

Prompt caching reduces input token cost to 10% for cache hits by storing the KV state of a static prefix on the provider's infrastructure; the one-time write cost on a cache miss is 1.25×, so break-even is reached after the second hit in a TTL window.
The cached prefix must be byte-for-byte identical between requests; any dynamic content — timestamps, user identifiers, session IDs — must appear after the cache breakpoint, not before it.
to the last static content block; the minimum prefix size is 2,048 tokens for Sonnet/Opus and 1,024 tokens for Haiku — requests below the minimum are silently uncached.
from every response; a sustained drop in hit rate is the fastest signal that a deploy has invalidated the cache or that traffic is too sparse.

Prompt caching and latency improvements compound with prefix size; agents with 8,000-token system prompts (tools + reference docs) can see both 90% cost reduction on the prefix and over one second of time-to-first-token improvement on cache hits.

Prompt caching, semantic caching, and model downgrading address different cost dimensions and are best combined: prompt caching reduces static prefix cost, semantic caching short-circuits repeated queries, and routing to a cheaper model reduces per-token cost for low-complexity calls.

Never embed user-supplied content before the cache breakpoint; anything placed in the cached prefix that a user can influence creates a prompt injection vector that affects all sessions sharing that cache entry.
